question.text:
Consider the pushdown automaton (PDA) P below, 
which runs on the input alphabet {a, b}, has stack 
alphabet {⊥, A}, and has three states {s, p, q}, with 
s being the start state. A transition from state u to 
state v, labelled c/X/γ, where c is an input symbol or 
, X is a stack symbol, and γ is a string of stack 
symbols, represents the fact that in state u, the PDA 
can read c from the input, with X on the top of its 
stack, pop X from the stack, push in the string γ on 
the stack, and go to state v. In the initial 
configuration, the stack has only the symbol ⊥ in it. 
The PDA accepts by empty stack.  


Which one of the following options correctly 
describes the language accepted by P?

solution.text:
State s can push A for each input a, and whenever b 
comes as input, it will pop A and then changes to 
state p. If end of the input happens from state s, then 
it will change to state q and will pop all A's to make 
empty stack. 

State p can pop A for each input b, and whenever 
end of input reaches, it will change to state q and 
pop all A's from stack to make empty stack. 

Case1: a* will be accepted if s and q are involved.  

Case2: am bn will be accepted (m > n  > 0) if s, p, and 
q are involved.  

L = {am bn | m > n \ge 0} = {am bn | m > n, m \ge 1} 

Therefore, option (a) is correct.

Consider the following pushdown automaton over 
the input alphabet \sum = {a, b} and stack alphabet Γ = 
{#, A}. 


The number of strings of length 100 accepted by the 
above pushdown automaton is _____.

solution.text:
Language by PDA = {am bn | m > n} 
• 
Sum of a's and b's must be 100 
• 
Possible strings are: a100 b0, a99 b1, a92 b2, …., 
a51 b49. 
• 
Total number of 100 length strings  


= 100 - 51 + 1 = 50

question.text:
Which one of the following grammars is free from 
left recursion?

options.text:
S \rightarrow Ab | Bb | c 


A \rightarrow Aa | b 

A \rightarrow Bd |  


B \rightarrow c 

B \rightarrow e

S \rightarrow Aa | Bb | c 


A \rightarrow Bb |Sc |  

A \rightarrow Bd |  


B \rightarrow d 

B \rightarrow Ae | 

solution.text:
(a) S \rightarrow AB 


A \rightarrow Aa | b 


B \rightarrow c 


A \rightarrow A there is a left recursion A to A. 

(b) S \rightarrow Ab | Bb | c 


A \rightarrow Bd |  


B \rightarrow e 


There is not left recursion. 

(c) S \rightarrow Aa | B 


A \rightarrow Bb |Sc |  


B \rightarrow d 


S \rightarrow A \rightarrow S indirectly there is a left 
recursion S to S. 

(d) S \rightarrow Aa | Bb | c 


A \rightarrow Bd |  


B \rightarrow Ae |  


A \rightarrow B \rightarrow A indirectly there is a left 
recursion A to A.

question.text:
Consider the following context-free grammars: 

G1: S \rightarrow aS | B 

       B \rightarrow b | bB 

G2: S \rightarrow aA | bB 

       A \rightarrow aA | B |  

       B \rightarrow bB |  

Which one of the following pairs of languages is 
generated by G1 and G2 respectively?

options.text:
{am bn│m > 0 or n > 0}  and {am bn |m >0 and 
n > 0}

{am bn│m > 0 and n > 0}  and {am bn |m > 0 or 
n \ge 0}

{am bn│m \ge 0 or n > 0}and {am bn |m > 0 and 
n > 0}

{am bn│m \ge 0 and n > 0}  and {am bn |m > 0 or 
n > 0}

solution.text:
• 
G1: S \rightarrow aS | B 

             B \rightarrow b | bB 

              B = b+ 

              S = a*b+ 

              L1 = {am bn│m \ge 0 and n > 0} 
• 
G2: S \rightarrow aA | bB 

             A \rightarrow aA | B |  

             B \rightarrow bB |  

             B = b* 

             A = a*b* 

             S = aa*b* | bb* 

             L2 = a+ b*  b+ 

            L2 = {am bn |m > 0 or n > 0} 


   Hence, option (d) is correct.

steps are required to derive the string "aabbaab" and 
how many parse trees are there?

A \rightarrow aAb | bAa |  


S \rightarrow aS \rightarrow aA \rightarrow aaAb \rightarrow aabAab \rightarrow aabbAaab 
 \rightarrow aabbaab

question.text:
Which of the following statements are true? 

1. Every left - recursive grammar can be 
converted to a right - recursive grammar 
and 
vice-versa 

2. All  - productions can be removed from any 
context - free grammar by  suitable 
transformations 

3. The language generated by a context-free 
grammar all of whose productions are of the 
form X \rightarrow  w or wY (where, w is a string of 
terminals and Y is a non-terminal), is always 
regular 

4. The derivation trees of strings generated by a 
context-free grammar in  Chomsky 
Normal 
Form are always binary trees

options.text:
1, 2 and 4 only 

Context Free Language Identification

solution.text:
1. Every left - recursive grammar can be 
converted to a right - recursive grammar and 
vice-versa. TRUE 

2. All  - productions can be removed from any 
context - free grammar by  suitable 
transformations. FALSE 

3. The language generated by a context-free 
grammar all of whose productions are of the 
form X \rightarrow  w or wY (where, w is a string of 
terminals and Y is a non-terminal), is always 
regular. TRUE 

4. The derivation trees of strings generated by a 
context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal 
Form are always binary trees. TRUE

question.text: Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: The language L ={an bn | n \ge 0} is regular.
The language L = {an | n is prime } is regular

The language L = {w |w has 3k + 1 b' s for 
some k  N with \sum = {a, b}} is regular.

The language L = {w w | w * with 
 = {0, 1}} is regular.

solution.text:
(a) The language L ={an bn | n \ge 0} is regular. 
False 

(b) The language L = {an | n is prime } is regular. 
False 

(c) The language L = {w |w has 3k + 1 b' s for 
some k  N with \sum = {a, b}} is regular. True 

(d) The language L = {w w | w * with 
 = {0, 1}} is regular. False

question.text:
Suppose that L1 is a regular language and L2 is a 
context-free language. Which one of the following 
languages as NOT necessarily context-free?

solution.text:
L1 \Rightarrow Regular  

 L2 \Rightarrow CFL 

(i)  L1   L2 \Rightarrow Reg  CFL \Rightarrow CFL 

(ii)  L1 . L2 \Rightarrow Reg  CFL \Rightarrow CFL 

(iii)  L1 - L2 \Rightarrow Reg - CFL \Rightarrow Reg  CFL \Rightarrow CSL 

(iv)  L1  L2 \Rightarrow Reg  CFL \Rightarrow CFL

question.text:
Consider the following languages over the alphabet 
Σ = {a, b, c}  

Let L1 = {an bn cm | m, n \ge 0} and 

      L2 ={ambncn | m, n \ge 0}. 

Which of the following are context-free languages?  

I.  L1  L2  
 II.  L1  L2

solution.text:
L1 = {an bn cm | m, n \ge 0} = CFL 

L2 ={am bn cn  | m, n \ge 0} = CFL 

 I. 
L1  L2 \Rightarrow CFL  CFL = CFL 

II. L1  L2 = {an bn c*   a* bn cn} 


             = {an bn cn | n \ge 0} \Rightarrow CSL

tags: Context Free Language and Context Free Grammar

question.text:
Let P be a regular language and Q be a context free 
language such that Q  P. (For example, let P be the 
language represented by the regular expression p*q* 
and Q be {pn qn | n  N}). Then which of the 
following is ALWAYS regular?

solution.text:
A. Regular  CFL \Rightarrow CFL 

B. Regular - CFL 


Regular  CFL \Rightarrow need not be regular 

 C. * - Regular \Rightarrow Regular 

 D. * - CFL \Rightarrow need not be regular 

  Hence, option (c) is correct.

questions.subject: Theory Of Computation
questions.topic: Context Free Language and Context Free Grammar
tags: Context Free Language and Context Free Grammar

question.text: Which one of the following is FALSE?

options.text:
There is a unique minimal DFA for every 
regular language.

Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent 
PDA.

Complement of every context-free language is 
recursive.

Every nondeterministic PDA can be converted 
to an equivalent deterministic PDA.

solution.text:
• 
For Regular language minimal DFA is unique. 
• 
Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent 
PDA. 
• 
CFL
CSL
=

Every CSL is recursive. 
• 
Every nondeterministic PDA can be converted 
to an equivalent deterministic PDA.
